title: Personal Financial Literacy: Money Skills for Everyday Life
subtitle: Build practical money habits to earn, save, budget, and invest with confidence
audience: African university students and young professionals
description: This beginner-friendly course gives African students and young professionals the essential skills to take control of their money. Through short, mobile-friendly sessions, you will learn how to budget, save, manage debt wisely, protect yourself from fraud, and start building wealth. Every lesson uses relatable African examples such as mobile money, side hustles, and family financial responsibilities, so you can apply what you learn immediately in daily life.
outcomes: Explain core personal finance concepts including income, expenses, needs versus wants, and net worth.
Create a realistic monthly budget using a simple method that fits variable or irregular income.
Apply practical saving and emergency-fund strategies suited to low and uncertain incomes.
Evaluate borrowing options and calculate the true cost of debt before taking a loan.
Identify common financial scams and describe steps to protect personal money and data.
Describe basic saving and investment options available in African markets to grow money over time.
competencies: Creating and maintaining a personal budget for variable income
Building consistent saving habits and an emergency fund
Understanding basic investment options in African markets

options: A plan for how you will earn and spend your money
A savings account with high interest
A tax paid to the government

questions.q: Which of these is an example of a 'need' rather than a 'want'?
options: Basic food and transport to work

questions.q: What does it usually mean when a loan has a high interest rate?
options: You repay less than you borrowed
You will repay much more than you borrowed
Interest has no effect on repayment

questions.q: In the 50/30/20 budgeting method, what does the 20% typically represent?
What is the main purpose of an emergency fund?
options: To invest in the stock market
To cover unexpected expenses without borrowing
To pay monthly rent in advance
Borrowing to fund purchases that lose value quickly
Borrowing that can build income or long-term value, like education
Debt with the highest possible interest rate

questions.q: You receive a message asking for your mobile money PIN to 'confirm a prize'. What should you do?
options: Share your PIN to claim the prize
Never share your PIN and report the message
Reply with your PIN but change it later

questions.q: Why does inflation matter for your savings?
options: It increases the value of cash over time
It has no effect on money
It reduces the buying power of money kept idle

sessions.title: Money Mindset and the Basics
objectives: Define income, expenses, assets, liabilities, and net worth in plain terms.
Distinguish between needs and wants using everyday examples.
Reflect on personal money habits and set clear financial goals.
sessions.text:
Money starts with five simple words. Income is money coming in: your salary, allowance, or side-hustle earnings. Expenses are money going out: transport, airtime, rent, food. Assets are things you own that hold or grow value: cash savings, a laptop you earn with, land, or shares. Liabilities are what you owe: a phone loan, borrowed school fees, unpaid credit. Net worth is simply assets minus liabilities — a snapshot of your financial health.

Try this now. On paper, list what you own and its value (assets), then list what you owe (liabilities). Subtract. Even if the number is small or negative, that's your starting point.

Next, separate needs from wants. A need keeps you functioning: food, transport to class, basic data. A want is nice but optional: a new sneaker, eating out, the latest phone. Before spending, ask: "Will this still matter next week?"

Finally, set one SMART goal — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Instead of "save more," write "save 15,000 in 3 months for an emergency fund." Break it down: that's 5,000 monthly, or about 165 daily. Small, tracked steps build real financial confidence.
sessions.voiceScript: Welcome to your first money lesson. Let's keep it simple. Think of money as flowing in and out of your life. What flows in is your income \u2014 your salary, allowance, or hustle. What flows out is your expenses \u2014 transport, food, airtime. Now, the things you own that hold value, like savings or a laptop, are your assets. The things you owe, like loans, are your liabilities. When you subtract what you owe from what you own, you get your net worth. That single number tells you where you stand today. Before you spend, gently ask yourself whether it's a need or a want. And this week, set just one clear, time-bound goal. You've got this.
keyPoints: Net worth = assets (what you own) minus liabilities (what you owe).
Income flows in; expenses flow out \u2014 track both.
Needs keep you functioning; wants are optional \u2014 pause before spending.
Set one SMART goal: specific, measurable, and time-bound.
sessions.whiteboardSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='0' width='480' height='260' fill='#ffffff'/><text x='140' y='28' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='18' fill='#0f766e'>Net Worth Formula</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='120' height='60' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='#15803d' stroke-width='3'/><text x='55' y='85' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='15' fill='#15803d'>ASSETS</text><text x='45' y='106' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#334155'>what you own</text><text x='165' y='98' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='28' fill='#334155'>-</text><rect x='195' y='60' width='120' height='60' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'/><text x='210' y='85' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='15' fill='#b45309'>LIABILITIES</text><text x='215' y='106' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#334155'>what you owe</text><text x='330' y='98' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='28' fill='#334155'>=</text><rect x='360' y='60' width='100' height='60' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='#0f766e' stroke-width='3'/><text x='372' y='95' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='14' fill='#0f766e'>NET WORTH</text><line x1='30' y1='160' x2='450' y2='160' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='185' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='13' fill='#15803d'>Income in -></text><text x='300' y='185' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='13' fill='#b45309'>-> Expenses out</text><circle cx='240' cy='220' r='16' fill='none' stroke='#0f766e' stroke-width='3'/><text x='150' y='225' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#334155'>Need or Want?</text></svg>
sessions.whiteboardCaption: A diagram showing net worth as assets minus liabilities, with income flowing in and expenses flowing out.
sessions.example: Amara, a Lagos undergrad, earns 40,000 monthly from tutoring. She owns 20,000 in savings and a 60,000 laptop (assets = 80,000) but owes 30,000 on a phone loan (liability). Her net worth is 50,000. She marks transport and data as needs, and skips a 15,000 party outfit (a want), redirecting it toward a 45,000 emergency-fund goal in three months.

quiz.q: Which of these is a liability?
Which is the best example of a SMART goal?

flashcards.back: Money coming in \u2014 salary, allowance, or side-hustle earnings.
Something you owe, such as a loan or unpaid credit.
Assets minus liabilities \u2014 a snapshot of financial health.
A need keeps you functioning; a want is optional and nice-to-have.

sessions.reflection: Looking at your last week of spending, which purchase was a want disguised as a need \u2014 and what would you do differently?
sessions.summary: You learned the five core money terms, how to calculate net worth, and how to tell needs from wants while setting one clear SMART financial goal.
sessions.discussionPrompt: Ask the EFIKO tutor: \"Help me calculate my personal net worth and turn it into a realistic 3-month savings goal based on my income.\"

sessions.title: Budgeting That Works for You
objectives: Track income and spending for a typical month.
Build a budget using the 50/30/20 method adapted for irregular income.
Adjust a budget to cover shortfalls and unexpected costs.
sessions.text:
A budget is simply a plan for your money before you spend it. Start by tracking: for one month, write down every naira, cedi, or shilling that comes in (income) and goes out (spending). Use a notebook or a phone note. Group spending into three buckets.

The 50/30/20 method splits your income: 50% to Needs (rent, food, transport, data for school), 30% to Wants (eating out, streaming, outings), and 20% to Savings and debt repayment. Example: if you earn 60,000 monthly, that's 30,000 needs, 18,000 wants, 12,000 savings.

Irregular income? Budget from your LOWEST recent month, not your best. Treat that low figure as your baseline. When a bigger month comes, send the extra straight to savings and an "emergency buffer."

How-to in 4 steps: (1) Add up last month's total income. (2) List and total your Needs. (3) Assign Wants and Savings from what remains. (4) Review weekly and adjust.

Shortfall? Cut Wants first, never Needs or your minimum savings. Unexpected cost, like a phone repair? Pull from your buffer, then rebuild it. A budget isn't a cage; it's a tool you reshape each month to match real life.
sessions.voiceScript: Let's make budgeting feel simple and kind, not scary. A budget is just a plan for your money before it disappears. So first, for one month, track everything coming in and everything going out. A phone note works fine. Then split your income three ways: about half for needs like rent, food and transport, roughly a third for the fun things you enjoy, and a fifth for savings. If your income jumps around, plan using your lowest month, and whenever a good month arrives, quietly move the extra into savings. When money runs short, trim your wants first, never your needs. And build a small buffer so surprises don't break you. You've got this.
keyPoints: Track all income and spending for one month first.
Split income 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings.
With irregular income, budget from your lowest month and save the surplus.
Fix shortfalls by cutting wants and using your buffer, never your needs.
sessions.whiteboardSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect width='480' height='260' fill='white'/><text x='140' y='30' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='18' fill='#334155'>50/30/20 Budget</text><circle cx='120' cy='150' r='70' fill='none' stroke='#0f766e' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M120 150 L120 80 A70 70 0 0 1 190 150 Z' fill='#0f766e' opacity='0.3'/><path d='M120 150 L190 150 A70 70 0 0 1 150 214 Z' fill='#15803d' opacity='0.3'/><path d='M120 150 L150 214 A70 70 0 1 1 120 80 Z' fill='#b45309' opacity='0.2'/><rect x='260' y='70' width='18' height='18' fill='#0f766e'/><text x='286' y='85' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='14' fill='#334155'>50% Needs</text><rect x='260' y='110' width='18' height='18' fill='#15803d'/><text x='286' y='125' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='14' fill='#334155'>30% Wants</text><rect x='260' y='150' width='18' height='18' fill='#b45309'/><text x='286' y='165' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='14' fill='#334155'>20% Savings</text><text x='40' y='245' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#334155'>Plan from your lowest income month</text></svg>
sessions.whiteboardCaption: A pie chart splitting income into 50% needs, 30% wants, and 20% savings.
sessions.example: Amara, a Lagos undergrad, tutors for irregular pay. Her lowest month was 40,000 naira, so she budgets on that: 20,000 needs, 12,000 wants, 8,000 savings. In a good month she earns 70,000 and moves the extra 30,000 into savings and a small emergency buffer, so a surprise phone repair never derails her studies.

quiz.q: In the 50/30/20 method, what does the 20% cover?
If your income is irregular, which month should you base your budget on?
When you face a shortfall, what should you cut first?

flashcards.back: A plan for your money made before you spend it.
50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings and debt repayment.
Plan from your lowest recent month; save any surplus from better months.
Savings set aside for unexpected costs, rebuilt after use.

sessions.reflection: Looking at last month, which spending 'want' could you trim to grow your savings by even 5%?
sessions.summary: Budgeting means tracking your money, then splitting it 50/30/20, adapting to irregular income by planning from your lowest month and cutting wants first when money is tight.
sessions.discussionPrompt: Help me build a realistic 50/30/20 budget for my actual monthly income and expenses, and suggest where to trim if I fall short.

sessions.title: Saving and Emergency Funds
objectives: Explain why an emergency fund matters and how much to target.
Apply the 'pay yourself first' habit using mobile money or savings groups.
Compare formal and informal saving options like banks, SACCOs, and ajo/esusu.
sessions.text:
An emergency fund is money set aside for surprises like a hospital bill, phone repair, or sudden transport costs. It stops you from borrowing at high interest or selling things you need. Start small: target one month of essential spending, then build toward three to six months. If your monthly essentials are 40,000 naira, your first goal is 40,000.

The key habit is 'pay yourself first': the moment money arrives, move a fixed amount to savings BEFORE spending. Do not wait to save 'what is left' because usually nothing is left.

How-to (mobile money): the day you get paid, transfer 10% to a separate wallet or locked savings pocket (like M-Pesa Lock, MoMo, or a savings app). Set an automatic reminder.

Worked example: You earn 30,000 monthly. Pay yourself first 10% = 3,000. In 12 months you save 36,000 plus any interest, close to one month's cushion.

Compare your options: a bank gives safety and cards but may charge fees; a SACCO or cooperative offers higher returns and loans to members; ajo/esusu (rotating savings) builds discipline through your group but earns no interest and depends on trust. Keep emergency money accessible, not locked for years, so you can reach it fast.
sessions.voiceScript: Let's talk about building a safety net, even on a small income. An emergency fund is simply money you keep for surprises, so a small crisis doesn't turn into debt. My favourite habit is called paying yourself first. The day money reaches you, before you spend a single naira or shilling, move a fixed slice into savings. If you wait for what's left over, honestly, nothing is left. Try ten percent into a separate mobile money pocket you don't touch. Start by aiming for one month of essential expenses, then grow toward three to six months. And choose where to keep it wisely, whether that's a bank, a SACCO, or your trusted ajo group. Small, steady, automatic. That's how safety nets get built.
keyPoints: Pay yourself first: save a fixed % before spending.
Target one month of essentials, then grow to 3-6 months.
Keep emergency money safe but easy to access.
Choose banks, SACCOs, or ajo/esusu based on safety, returns, and trust.
sessions.whiteboardSvg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 260'><rect width='480' height='260' fill='#ffffff'/><text x='20' y='30' fill='#0f766e' font-size='18' font-family='sans-serif'>Pay Yourself First</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='90' height='50' rx='6' fill='none' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><text x='45' y='90' fill='#334155' font-size='13' font-family='sans-serif'>Income</text><line x1='120' y1='85' x2='190' y2='85' stroke='#15803d' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M185 80 L195 85 L185 90' fill='none' stroke='#15803d' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='195' y='55' width='100' height='40' rx='6' fill='none' stroke='#15803d' stroke-width='2'/><text x='210' y='80' fill='#15803d' font-size='13' font-family='sans-serif'>Save 10%</text><line x1='120' y1='95' x2='190' y2='120' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M183 112 L192 120 L181 122' fill='none' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='195' y='105' width='100' height='40' rx='6' fill='none' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='2'/><text x='208' y='130' fill='#b45309' font-size='13' font-family='sans-serif'>Spend rest</text><text x='20' y='185' fill='#0f766e' font-size='15' font-family='sans-serif'>Target: 1 month then 3-6 months</text><line x1='30' y1='205' x2='450' y2='205' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='195' width='80' height='10' fill='#15803d'/><rect x='110' y='195' width='240' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#334155' stroke-width='1'/><text x='30' y='230' fill='#334155' font-size='12' font-family='sans-serif'>1mo</text><text x='330' y='230' fill='#334155' font-size='12' font-family='sans-serif'>6mo goal</text></svg>
sessions.whiteboardCaption: A flow showing income split into savings first and spending after, with a target bar from one to six months.
sessions.example: Chidi, a Lagos graduate earning 60,000 naira monthly, sets up an automatic transfer of 6,000 to a locked MoMo savings pocket each payday. He also joins his office ajo for discipline. After ten months he has 60,000 saved; when his laptop charger dies before a job interview, he buys a replacement instantly instead of borrowing.

options: Move savings aside before spending anything else
Only save what remains at month end

quiz.q: What is a sensible first emergency-fund target?
Which saving option typically earns NO interest but builds group discipline?

flashcards.back: Money set aside for surprises so you avoid borrowing at high interest.
Transfer a fixed savings amount the moment income arrives, before spending.
Start with one month of essentials, build toward three to six months.
A savings cooperative offering members higher returns and access to loans.

sessions.reflection: What fixed percentage of your next income can you move to savings before you spend anything, and where will you keep it?
sessions.summary: An emergency fund built by paying yourself first protects you from debt; start with one month of essentials and choose a saving option that balances safety, returns, and easy access.
sessions.discussionPrompt: Help me compare keeping my emergency fund in a bank, a SACCO, and an ajo group based on my income and how quickly I might need the money.

sessions.title: Smart Borrowing and Managing Debt
objectives: Identify good debt versus bad debt.
Calculate the real cost of a loan including interest and fees.
Create a plan to repay existing debts and avoid over-borrowing.
sessions.text:
Borrowing is a tool. Used wisely it builds your future; used carelessly it traps you. Good debt buys something that grows in value or income, like a laptop for freelancing or a school fee that raises your earning power. Bad debt funds things that lose value fast, like clothes or airtime bought on credit.

Always calculate the REAL cost, not just the sticker rate. Total cost = amount borrowed + interest + all fees. Example: a digital lender offers 10,000 for 30 days at '5% interest' plus a 500 processing fee. Interest = 5% x 10,000 = 500. Total repay = 10,000 + 500 + 500 = 11,000. You paid 1,000 to borrow 10,000 for one month. Annualised, that is roughly 120% per year, very expensive.

To repay existing debts, list every loan with its balance and interest rate. Use the AVALANCHE method: pay minimums on all, then throw extra money at the HIGHEST-rate debt first. It saves the most money. Keep total monthly debt payments under 30% of your income.

Before borrowing, ask: Do I need it? Can I repay comfortably? What is the total cost? If unsure, wait and save instead.
sessions.voiceScript: Let's talk about borrowing, because a loan is really just a tool. Used wisely, it can build your future; used carelessly, it can trap you. So first, I want you to separate good debt from bad debt. Good debt buys something that grows your income or value, like a laptop for freelance work. Bad debt funds things that vanish quickly. Then, always check the true cost. If you borrow ten thousand for a month and pay back eleven thousand, that extra thousand is the real price, and annualised it's shockingly high. When repaying, list every loan and attack the highest interest rate first. And before you ever borrow, ask yourself honestly: do I truly need this, and can I repay it comfortably?
keyPoints: Good debt grows income or value; bad debt funds things that lose value.
Real cost = amount borrowed + interest + all fees, not just the advertised rate.
Use the avalanche method: clear highest-interest debt first.
Keep total monthly debt payments under 30% of your income.
sessions.whiteboardSvg: <svg viewBox="0 0 480 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect width="480" height="260" fill="#ffffff"/><text x="20" y="28" font-size="16" fill="#334155">Real Cost of a Loan</text><rect x="30" y="50" width="120" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#0f766e" stroke-width="2"/><text x="42" y="75" font-size="13" fill="#0f766e">Borrow 10000</text><line x1="150" y1="70" x2="200" y2="70" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="200" y="40" width="110" height="30" fill="none" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="2"/><text x="212" y="60" font-size="12" fill="#b45309">Interest 500</text><rect x="200" y="78" width="110" height="30" fill="none" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="2"/><text x="212" y="98" font-size="12" fill="#b45309">Fee 500</text><line x1="310" y1="70" x2="360" y2="70" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="360" y="50" width="100" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2"/><text x="368" y="75" font-size="12" fill="#15803d">Repay 11000</text><text x="20" y="150" font-size="14" fill="#334155">Avalanche repayment:</text><line x1="30" y1="165" x2="440" y2="165" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="30" y="175" width="140" height="55" fill="none" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="2"/><text x="40" y="197" font-size="12" fill="#b45309">Highest rate</text><text x="40" y="215" font-size="12" fill="#b45309">pay first</text><rect x="190" y="175" width="120" height="55" fill="none" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2"/><text x="200" y="205" font-size="12" fill="#334155">then next</text><rect x="330" y="175" width="120" height="55" fill="none" stroke="#0f766e" stroke-width="2"/><text x="340" y="205" font-size="12" fill="#0f766e">keep <30%</text></svg>
sessions.whiteboardCaption: A diagram showing how a 10,000 loan grows to 11,000 with interest and fees, plus the avalanche repayment order.
sessions.example: Amara, a student in Lagos, borrows 10,000 naira from a mobile lending app for 30 days at 5% interest plus a 500 naira fee. She repays 11,000, meaning she paid 1,000 to use 10,000 for one month. Realising this equals about 120% per year, she decides next time to save for two weeks instead and skip the loan entirely.

quiz.q: Which of these is most likely GOOD debt?
options: A loan to buy new party clothes
A loan for a laptop to earn freelance income
A payday loan for a night out

quiz.q: You borrow 10,000 at 5% interest plus a 500 fee. What is the total you repay?
In the avalanche repayment method, you pay extra toward which debt first?

flashcards.back: Good debt buys things that grow income/value (e.g. education, work tools); bad debt funds items that quickly lose value.
Amount borrowed + interest + all fees. Compare this total, not just the advertised interest rate.
Pay minimums on all debts, then put extra money toward the highest-interest debt first to save the most.
Keep total monthly debt repayments under about 30% of your income.

sessions.reflection: Think about any money you currently owe or a purchase you were tempted to finance. Was it good or bad debt, and what was the true cost?
sessions.summary: Borrow only for things that build your future, always calculate the full cost including fees, and repay strategically by tackling the highest-interest debt first while keeping payments under 30% of income.
sessions.discussionPrompt: Ask the EFIKO tutor to help you compare two real digital loan offers and work out which one is genuinely cheaper once fees are included.

sessions.title: Protecting Your Money from Fraud
objectives: Recognise common scams targeting mobile money and bank users.
List practical steps to secure PINs, passwords, and personal data.
Respond correctly if you suspect fraud or unauthorised transactions.
sessions.text:
Fraudsters target money you can't see them steal. Learn their playbook so you spot it early.

Common scams: (1) Phishing SMS or calls pretending to be your bank or mobile-money provider, asking for your PIN, OTP (one-time password sent to your phone), or card number. (2) "You've won" or "send a small fee to receive" messages. (3) Fake agents who ask you to reverse a wrong transfer. (4) SIM-swap, where a criminal takes over your phone number to intercept OTPs.

Golden rule: NO legitimate bank or telco EVER asks for your PIN or OTP. Never share them, even with someone claiming to be staff.

How to secure yourself (do this today):
1. Set a strong, unique PIN/password; avoid 1234 or your birth year.
2. Enable two-factor authentication on banking and email apps.
3. Never save PINs in your phone notes.
4. Verify links: type your bank's address yourself instead of clicking.
5. Cover the keypad when entering your PIN at ATMs and agents.

If you suspect fraud: act fast. Call your bank's official hotline (from the card back, not the SMS), freeze or block the account/SIM, change passwords, and report to the provider and police. Keep records of dates, amounts, and messages.
sessions.voiceScript: Let me share something that could save you real money. Fraudsters rely on urgency and trust, so they'll text or call pretending to be your bank, telling you to act now and share your PIN or one-time password. Here's the rule I want you to remember forever: no genuine bank or mobile-money company will ever ask for your PIN or OTP. If someone does, it's a scam, full stop. Protect yourself with simple habits, use a strong unique PIN, turn on two-factor authentication, and never click suspicious links. And if something feels wrong, don't panic and don't stay silent. Call your bank's official number, block the account, and report it quickly. Speed protects your money.
keyPoints: No real bank or telco ever asks for your PIN or OTP, share them with no one.
Use strong unique PINs, enable two-factor authentication, and verify links before clicking.
Watch for phishing, prize scams, reversal tricks, and SIM-swap fraud.
If defrauded, act fast: call the official hotline, block the account, and report it.
sessions.whiteboardSvg: <svg viewBox="0 0 480 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect width="480" height="260" fill="#ffffff"/><text x="20" y="30" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="16" fill="#0f766e">Protect Your Money</text><rect x="20" y="50" width="180" height="70" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30" y="75" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#b45309">SCAM SMS</text><text x="30" y="95" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#334155">"Send your OTP</text><text x="30" y="110" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#334155">to claim prize!"</text><line x1="200" y1="85" x2="270" y2="85" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M262 78 L270 85 L262 92" fill="none" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="330" cy="85" r="35" fill="none" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="305" y1="110" x2="355" y2="60" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="3"/><text x="300" y="140" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#b45309">Never share PIN/OTP</text><text x="20" y="175" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#15803d">Safe habits:</text><line x1="25" y1="190" x2="35" y2="200" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="35" y1="200" x2="50" y2="183" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2"/><text x="60" y="198" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#334155">Strong unique PIN</text><line x1="25" y1="213" x2="35" y2="223" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="35" y1="223" x2="50" y2="206" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2"/><text x="60" y="221" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#334155">Two-factor login</text><line x1="25" y1="236" x2="35" y2="246" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="35" y1="246" x2="50" y2="229" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2"/><text x="60" y="244" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#334155">Verify, then act fast</text></svg>
sessions.whiteboardCaption: A scam text asking for an OTP is crossed out, alongside three safe-money habits.
sessions.example: Amara, a university student in Lagos, gets an SMS: "Your bank account is blocked. Reply with your PIN and OTP to reactivate." She remembers no bank asks for these. She ignores the SMS, dials the number printed on the back of her debit card, and confirms her account is fine. By verifying instead of reacting, she avoids losing her semester allowance.

quiz.q: A caller says they are from your bank and need your OTP to 'verify' your account. What should you do?
options: Give the OTP since they called you
Refuse, hang up, and call the official bank number yourself
Give only half the OTP to be safe
Text the OTP instead of saying it

quiz.q: Which is the strongest mobile-money PIN choice?
options: A random unique 4-6 digit number you don't reuse
A criminal taking over your phone number to intercept OTPs

flashcards.back: A one-time password sent to your phone to authorise a transaction; never share it with anyone.

flashcards.front: The golden rule of fraud protection
flashcards.back: No legitimate bank or telco ever asks for your PIN or OTP.
Fake messages or calls pretending to be a trusted institution to trick you into revealing secrets.

flashcards.front: First step if you suspect fraud
flashcards.back: Call your bank's official hotline (from your card, not the SMS) and block the account fast.

sessions.reflection: Which of your current money habits (PINs, saved passwords, clicking links) could a fraudster exploit, and what will you change today?
sessions.summary: Fraudsters exploit urgency and trust to steal money you can't see leaving; protect yourself by never sharing PINs or OTPs, using strong unique credentials with two-factor authentication, and responding fast through official channels if you suspect fraud.
sessions.discussionPrompt: Ask the EFIKO tutor to role-play a realistic scam call so I can practise recognising red flags and responding safely.

sessions.title: Growing Your Money: Saving and Investing Basics
objectives: Explain how interest and inflation affect money over time.
Compare beginner investment options such as savings accounts, treasury bills, and mutual funds.
Outline a simple first step to start investing safely.
sessions.text:
Money left idle loses value because of inflation, the steady rise in prices. If prices rise 15% a year and your cash earns nothing, your money buys 15% less next year. Investing fights this by earning a return.

Two forces matter. Interest is money your money earns. Compound interest means you earn returns on both your savings and past returns, so growth speeds up over time. Inflation is the eroding force. Your real goal: a return higher than inflation.

Worked example: You save 100,000 naira (or shillings/cedis) at 10% compound interest. After year 1 you have 110,000. After year 2, interest is charged on 110,000, giving 121,000, not 120,000. That extra 1,000 is compounding at work.

Compare beginner options:
- Savings account: safe, easy access, low return (may lag inflation).
- Treasury bills: short-term government loans, very safe, fixed return, often beats savings.
- Mutual funds: pooled money managed for you across many assets; higher potential return, more risk, small starting amounts.

Safe first step: build a small emergency fund first, then open a regulated money-market or treasury-bill account through a licensed provider. Start small, automate a monthly amount, and reinvest returns.
sessions.voiceScript: Let me leave you with the idea that money sitting still actually shrinks, because inflation quietly raises prices every year. So our job is to make money grow faster than prices rise. The magic tool here is compound interest, where you earn returns not just on what you saved, but also on the returns you already earned. Over the years, that snowballs. Think about your options like a ladder: a savings account is safe but slow, treasury bills are safe and usually beat it, and mutual funds pool your money for higher growth with more risk. My advice? Build a small emergency cushion, then start small with a licensed provider, automate it, and let time do the heavy lifting.
keyPoints: Inflation shrinks idle cash; aim for returns higher than inflation.
Compound interest earns returns on your returns, accelerating growth over time.
Savings accounts are safe but slow; treasury bills safer with better yield; mutual funds higher return but more risk.
Start safe: emergency fund first, then automate small deposits with a licensed provider.
sessions.whiteboardSvg: <svg viewBox="0 0 480 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect width="480" height="260" fill="#ffffff"/><text x="20" y="28" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="16" fill="#334155">Money over time</text><line x1="50" y1="210" x2="450" y2="210" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="210" x2="50" y2="50" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M50 200 Q250 190 450 175" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="3" fill="none"/><text x="360" y="168" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#b45309">inflation erodes</text><path d="M50 200 Q250 150 450 70" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="3" fill="none"/><text x="330" y="64" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#15803d">compound growth</text><circle cx="50" cy="200" r="4" fill="#0f766e"/><text x="22" y="230" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#334155">now</text><text x="420" y="230" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#334155">years</text><text x="70" y="110" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="12" fill="#0f766e">Goal: return > inflation</text></svg>
sessions.whiteboardCaption: A graph showing invested money compounding upward while idle cash is eroded by inflation over time.
sessions.example: Amara, a Lagos graduate, saves 20,000 naira monthly. Instead of leaving it in a zero-interest wallet where inflation eats it, she opens a regulated treasury-bill fund earning about 18%. After building a small emergency fund, she automates the deposit and reinvests each payout, watching compounding grow her balance.

quiz.q: Why does money left as idle cash lose value over time?
options: Because inflation raises prices, reducing purchasing power
Because interest rates are always negative
It only pays interest on your original deposit
You earn returns on both savings and previous returns

quiz.q: Which is generally the safest first investing step for a beginner?
options: Putting all savings into one company's shares
Borrowing to invest in mutual funds
Building an emergency fund, then using a licensed treasury-bill or money-market account
Keeping everything in cash at home

flashcards.back: The steady rise in prices over time, which reduces the purchasing power of money.
Earning returns on both your original money and the returns it has already generated.
A short-term loan to the government; very safe with a fixed return, usually beating a savings account.
Your investment return after subtracting inflation; the goal is a positive real return.

sessions.reflection: How much could you comfortably automate each month toward an investment, and where would that money currently be sitting instead?
sessions.summary: Investing helps your money grow faster than inflation through compound interest; beginners can start safely with an emergency fund, then treasury bills or mutual funds via a licensed provider.
sessions.discussionPrompt: Help me compare the current treasury-bill rates and inflation in my country, and design a simple monthly investing plan on my income.

spec.audience: African university students and young professionals
